stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( s \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad s \leftarrow s + i^{2} \] What value of \( s \) does the procedure output?
distractor_rationales: This adds \( i \) rather than \( i^{2} \) at each step, giving the triangular number.
This uses the sum-of-cubes formula \( \left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^{2} \) by mistake.
This keeps only the final term \( [[n]]^{2} \) instead of the running total.
This divides by 3 instead of 6, doubling the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( p \) is initialized to 1: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad p \leftarrow p \cdot i \] What value of \( p \) does the procedure output?
solution: The loop multiplies \( p \) by each \( i \) from 1 to [[n]] in turn, so \( p = 1 \cdot 2 \cdots [[n]] = [[n]]! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the running product with \( n^{n} \), multiplying by \( n \) each time instead of by the current index.
This stops the loop one iteration early, omitting the final multiplication by [[n]].
This runs one extra iteration beyond [[n]].
This mistakes the multiplicative accumulation for the additive triangular-number formula.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( s \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad \textbf{if } i \bmod 2 = 1: \quad s \leftarrow s + i \] What value of \( s \) does the procedure output?
solution: The condition keeps only the odd \( i \), namely \( 1, 3, \ldots \) up to [[n]]; there are \( \lceil [[n]]/2 \rceil \) of them, and the sum of the first \( k \) odd numbers is \( k^{2} \), so \( s = (\lceil [[n]]/2 \rceil)^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This approximates the count of odd terms as \( n/2 \) without rounding, squaring a non-integer count.
This uses \( \lfloor n/2 \rfloor \) instead of \( \lceil n/2 \rceil \), undercounting the odd terms when [[n]] is odd.
This forgets the filter and sums every \( i \) from 1 to [[n]], not just the odd ones.
This applies the sum-of-first-\(n\)-odd-numbers formula to all of [[n]], ignoring that only about half the terms pass the filter.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Consider the following procedure: \( x \) is initialized to [[n]] and \( c \) is initialized to 0. \[ \textbf{while } x > 1: \quad x \leftarrow \lfloor x/2 \rfloor, \quad c \leftarrow c + 1 \] What value of \( c \) does the procedure output when it terminates?
solution: Each pass halves \( x \) (rounding down), so \( x \) drops below or to 1 after exactly \( \lfloor \log_2 [[n]] \rfloor \) passes; hence \( c = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This rounds the halving count up instead of down, which overcounts unless [[n]] is an exact power of 2.
This confuses the number of halvings with the value \( x \) takes after just one halving.
This assumes each pass only decreases \( x \) by 1 rather than halving it.
This stops one iteration too early.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( p \) is initialized to 1: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad p \leftarrow p \cdot 2 \] What value of \( p \) does the procedure output?
solution: Each pass doubles \( p \), so after [[n]] passes \( p = 2^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes repeated doubling for repeated addition of 2.
This confuses the base and the exponent, squaring [[n]] instead of exponentiating 2.
This stops the loop one iteration early.
This runs one extra iteration beyond [[n]].
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
